sentences.text: My present name is Manusowicz.
M-A-- Should I spell it?
M-A-N-U-S-O-W-I-C-Z.
Manus.
M-A-N-U-S.
1930, 7th of October.
Today? 67.
Nearly.
Werba.
W-E-R-B-A.
Well, the largest would be Rowno.
R-O-W-N-O.
Poland.
Our family consisted-- the father's name was Nathan.
You want me to spell it as well?
No?
And the mother's name, Nechama.
Then I'll start for the oldest brother, that was Shlomo.
Second brother, [?
Etsach. ?] And my name is Fred.
Then we had a little baby sister was born in 1939, was Faiga.
Faigele.
Faiga.
Like a bird, Faiga.
Approximately, I would say 1897.
1897, approximately.
Also approximately, 1925 or 1926.
Etsach, ?] 1928.
And the little girl was born '39.
Well, we were a small town of about a little under 100 Jewish families, which probably was half of the population of the town.
And majority of the Jewish population, they were tailors.
Is it OK to-- Shoemakers, carpenters, they were mostly tradesmen.
And also, some small businesses.
Well, like in any other nationality, probably, there was-- I couldn't tell you, I was probably too young-- but there was some probably belonged to Communist party.
But the majority of our Jewish population was Zionists.
Maybe three different phrases of Zionist, like Betar and then, um, what's the-- labor.
Jewish labor.
But all connected with Zionists.
Well, we had, uh, shul and the Beth Midrash.
In other words, two Jewish synagogues.
And we also had, in the synagogue, a Hebrew school for children.
So we had two Jewish teachers in that-- in our little town.
Beth Midrash.
Well, not really.
Maybe the Beth Midrash was a little more religious, but not much.
Well, it was split.
For example, if I went to public school in the morning, then I went in the afternoon to the Hebrew school.
Or vice versa.
Like, um, in our town, we only had one public school.
So therefore, there was two different sessions.
The older kids used to go first thing in the morning, and the younger ones used to go later in the afternoon, according to the-- to the darkness of the-- the darkness of the day.
So if I went to morning public school, then afternoons I went to Hebrew school.
Well, we had a little shoe store.
Very little.
It-- it was in the house.
In them days, when somebody wanted to make a pair of shoes, they went to the shoemaker.
The shoemaker told him what size, and my father used to cut the materials, sell it to the guy, and he took it back to the shoemaker to make a pair of shoes.
Also, my father was traveling the farms.
He was a grain buyer for a company.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] also, by him traveling the farms, in the summer holidays he used to take the kids with him.
So we used to travel all over the country, the farms.
And that helped us survive after, by knowing the country so well, by traveling all the farms, the woods.
And--
Well, mother would be looking after it.
And in a small town, if they-- in them days, if they needed somebody, they waited.
Father always came home the same day.
I couldn't tell you.
We were all dreaming of Zionism.
But which?
I would say, probably, he was close more with the Labor movement.
None well, but probably Jewish was the main language and, uh, at home.
Yiddish, yes.
Well, probably my second language would be English, and Ukrainian, and Russian.
Not fanatically religious.
We were considered [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] conservative.
But we were very kosher in the house.
Observed.
Very kosher place.
We went every day to synagogue.
Not us kids, but the father went every day.
We went every Sabbath and Friday night.
The whole family.
Mother-- in them days, women didn't go as much to synagogue.
Mostly on holidays.
No, separately, on the balcony.
So what we would probably consider Orthodox today.
In them days, everybody was nearly Orthodox, kind of.
It-- every 99% of our town, we were observing the Kashrut, which is kosher food.
And probably 90% of the men went to synagogue every day.
Well, I started school in-- when I was 5 and 1/2, in public school.
That was Polish school.
And then I attended the pri-- the Hebrew school.
Or vice versa.
Half-- half day split, or maybe the Hebrew school was a little shorter.
Like our town was always-- they were educating us towards immigrating to Palestine.
So Hebrew was a first, a must.
And then it was religion, like the, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, let's say religion.
And how to write Hebrew, and how to speak Hebrew, and-- and how to pray, and--
Well, it's-- my childhood didn't come about.
But for whatever I had up until-- we played.
Like-- we were studying quite a bit of the day.
And-- but we did have a little activities, like playing maybe soccer, volleyball, and other athletic things.
And--
I really couldn't tell you, because, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The parents pushed us towards education.
Every-- and mainly in Hebrew education.
So-- but I actually-- maybe engineering would have been the closest thing that I was connected with.
Well, it wasn't the worst, but it-- we as Jews, we associated mostly with Jewish kids.
Very little that we played with other nationalities.
And, uh, it wasn't easy for a Jewish kid to walk on the street.
You were harassed, and-- not always, not by everybody, but we were harassed.
So we were always sticking together, between the Jewish kids.
Because my name was Ephraim, and Jewish-- being Jewish.
Yes.
Yes.
Not openly, but quite a bit.
Not really, no.
Not from the teachers, no.
In 1933, it didn't affect me.
I was only three years old.
And all I can tell you is what I read, which is probably-- it didn't affect in 1933, no.
After.
[PAUSES] When the Nazis--
When we started hearing about them, we were very worried and very scared.
And, uh, we were very happy when the Soviet Union invaded our country and we fell under their government instead of the German government.
So that saved us an extra two years of-- of hardship.
Well, we were scared of the Soviets also.
Because the Soviets, if-- majority of Jewish people, they couldn't own land.
So they were in business, and the Soviets were against the Jewish people that were in business.
They sent them to Siberia.
Our name was on the list to go to Siberia later on.
So we would've been lucky if we would have gone to Siberia.
We would have survived, the whole family.
So-- But we weren't too comfortable with the Soviets, because every day we were in the central rail.
Our town was on-- on the central rail lines.
And every day, there was Jewish business people with families transported to Siberia.
My mother always used to give me a little bag of food.
Take it to the train, and give it to them people.
We felt sorry for them.
But as it worked out, that's the only people that were lucky.
They survived by sending them to Siberia.
But at that time, we were not too comfortable with the Soviets, neither.
My father tried to run away when the Germans were coming closer to our village, but it was too late.
The Soviets informed us that they were taking the Germans, they're winning the war.
They didn't tell us that the Germans are next door already.
So the propaganda was that they're winning.
And so we didn't-- the Germans came in so quickly, we didn't have a chance to-- to run away.
The Germans?
That's when hell started.
Well, I would-- should we start from the beginning?
That will take me two hours.
Yeah.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the Germans came in, the first thing that I can remember-- I was 11 years old, approximately-- and the first hardship they did is every Jew had to wear a yellow badge on the front, and one in the back.
They shamed us in front of the Gentile people walking the street.
We were recognized.
And secondly, they took us out of school.
A Jew couldn't go to public school.
Couldn't go to any school.
We went-- under hiding, we went to the Hebrew school, which it was in no longer in a synagogue, but was in a private house of the-- of the teacher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Germans was in from '41.
The war started in '41, in about June.
And they were there until March '44.
And, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was lots of different things that went through, like our parents told us that there's going to be hardships.
And you were scared to walk on the street when the Germans came in.
We were always hiding in the house.
And after that, they put us in the ghetto.
It wasn't a fenced in ghetto.
It was a ghetto.
They put us-- they took two Jewish streets and packed in all the Jews from the whole town into that ghetto.
Also, they brought in other Jews from the smaller villages or farms, brought them into that ghetto.
So things were cramped, very, very cramped.
No name.
Werba ghetto, that's it.
We could, because there was no fences.
But after the Germans came in, they set up a local militia.
They were pro-Nazi.
Pro-Nazi, and, uh, and, uh, in some cases, they were worse than the Nazis.
Because they knew the Jewish population.
They knew them by first name, by name, by last name.
And they were a little harder on us than the Nazis.

Also the parents, I can remember, they pointed out to us that if you see Gestapo-- that was an elite German Nazi troop-- and they were-- and the Gestapo is the one that used to come and kill the Jews.
So they pointed out to us, if you see Gestapo, run.
Local Polish population, they were in the same position as us.
It's the Ukrainian.
This is Ukraine, and when the Nazis come in, the Poles weren't any longer.
It was Ukrainian Nazi supporters.
They were-- they were not treated well, but treated much better than the Jews.
They weren't killed, but they were, um, mistreated in a way.
Because the Polish government, they were ruling the Ukrainians, and then the Ukrainians started ruling and they mistreated the Poles.
Vice versa.
We were, because there was no fences.
It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- Father used to-- it wasn't very strict that you couldn't get out.
Like, Father used to go out to the farms, sneak out and bring some food.
Trade some stuff for food.
It-- yes.
You had to go to the farms.
Other people, too, yeah.
Because the Jewish stores, they-- they closed.
So you couldn't buy any flour or any-- any everyday needs.
No.
We didn't have apartments, none of them.
So, houses.
I really don't remember.
In our original house or in the ghetto?
In the ghetto.
It was-- it was so cramped.
And they set up the ghetto, they tried to [PAUSES] trick us, the Nazis, you know.
So they set up the ghettos, working people ghetto on one side.
The other side was business, or-- or one side was tradesmen, the other side was-- And like the parents figured that they wouldn't touch the tradesmen.
If they do anything, they'll touch first the, uh, the business.
So we stayed in the tradesmen section of the ghetto.
And it was very cramped, like they brought in the extra-- the whole town into two streets, plus the-- from the surrounding district.
So it was-- could have been 10 in a room, 12 in a room.
Rations, they didn't provide.
You were on your own.
Couldn't work, no.
A Jew lost all his privileges when the Nazis came in.
You couldn't-- they did take the work, they-- for Nazi type of work, which is things that weren't pleasant.
